answer:
# Declaring `--verbose` (root) and `--dry-run` (child)

## Root: persistent flag
Register on `PersistentFlags()` of the root command. Persistent flags are inherited by every descendant in the tree.

```go
var verbose bool

rootCmd := &cobra.Command{Use: "myapp", /* ... */}
rootCmd.PersistentFlags().BoolVarP(&verbose, "verbose", "v", false,
    "enable verbose output")
```

Any nested subcommand can read `--verbose` via `cmd.Flags().GetBool("verbose")` once parsing is done â€” the flag is merged into the executing command's `Flags()` set during `execute()`.

## Child: local-to-subtree flag
Register on the child's own `Flags()` if you want it scoped to that command, or on its `PersistentFlags()` if you want descendants of that child to inherit it too. For "only that child and its descendants":

```go
var dryRun bool

applyCmd := &cobra.Command{Use: "apply", /* ... */}
applyCmd.PersistentFlags().BoolVar(&dryRun, "dry-run", false,
    "show what would happen without doing it")

rootCmd.AddCommand(applyCmd)
```

If `apply` has no children and you don't want any descendants to inherit it, use `applyCmd.Flags()` instead of `PersistentFlags()` â€” this scopes the flag to `apply` alone.

---

# The four FlagSet accessors

Per the cobra source (ADR-02), a `Command` owns **five** internal `pflag.FlagSet`s â€” `flags`, `pflags`, `lflags`, `iflags`, and `parentsPflags` â€” each with a distinct role. The four public accessors are views over those:

| Method | What it returns | When you use it |
|---|---|---|
| **`Flags()`** | The fully-resolved flag set for *this* command after persistent flags from ancestors have been merged in. This is what argument parsing uses, and what you read at runtime inside `Run`/`RunE`. | Reading flag values during execution; registering a flag local to this command. |
| **`PersistentFlags()`** | Flags declared on this command that are inherited by all descendants. Stored separately from `Flags()` â€” they only show up in `Flags()` after the merge during `execute()`. | Declaring inheritable flags like `--verbose` on the root. |
| **`LocalFlags()`** | A computed view of flags that *belong to this command* â€” i.e. its own `Flags()` plus its own `PersistentFlags()`, but **excluding** flags inherited from ancestors. | Help/usage templates that show "Flags:" vs "Global Flags:". This is part of the public template API (ADR-05), so renaming would be breaking. |
| **`InheritedFlags()`** | A computed view of only the persistent flags inherited from ancestor commands. The complement of `LocalFlags()`. | Help/usage templates that render the "Global Flags:" section. |

### Two important consequences from ADR-02 / ADR-06

1. **`PersistentFlags()` and `Flags()` are separate sets.** A flag you put on `PersistentFlags()` will *not* appear in `Flags()` until cobra merges it during command execution. If you call something like `MarkFlagRequired` from your own setup code that needs an ancestor's persistent flag visible, cobra's flag-group machinery deliberately calls `mergePersistentFlags()` first (ADR-06) â€” you may need the same trick.

2. **Flag groups can't cross command boundaries** (ADR-06): all flags in a `MarkFlagsMutuallyExclusive` / `MarkFlagsRequiredTogether` group must be visible on a single `Command`'s merged flag set. You can't group a parent-persistent flag with a sibling's local flag.

### Mental model

- Define on **`PersistentFlags()`** â†’ inherited downward.
- Define on **`Flags()`** â†’ visible only to this command.
- Read with **`Flags()`** at runtime.
- Use **`LocalFlags()`** / **`InheritedFlags()`** only when you're customizing help or usage templates.

args.description: Find flag accessor method signatures in command.go
